meta.title: Neurology Practice Software | EMG/EEG Coordination, Infusion Management & Prior Auth | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to neurology practice operations. Coordinate EMG and EEG scheduling automatically. Manage MS and IVIG infusions. Automate prior auth for CGRP inhibitors and DMTs. Bill RTM for chronic neurology patients.

hero.subhead: Neurology practices lose revenue in three places: ancillary testing that never gets scheduled, infusion suites sitting half-full while coordinators chase authorizations, and chronic patients slipping between visits. clinIQ closes every gap — from EMG coordination to MS infusion to post-visit RTM billing.

RTM Billing for Chronic Neurology Patients

sections.content:
Neurology is one of the most administratively complex specialties in medicine. The average neurology practice manages three or four distinct patient populations simultaneously — chronic migraine patients, MS and autoimmune neurology patients, epilepsy patients, and general neurology cases — each with its own scheduling requirements, authorization rules, and follow-up cadence.

The complexity compounds at every step. Your MS patients need infusions every four to twelve weeks, and each infusion cycle requires a fresh authorization from payers who deny on any pretext. Your epilepsy patients need EEGs that your practice may or may not perform in-house, and if you send them to an external facility, coordination gaps mean results arrive two weeks after the follow-up appointment. Your chronic migraine patients are on CGRP inhibitors that cost $600–$900 per month, and every refill authorization requires documentation of previous medication failures across three to five drug classes.

The administrative burden falls on a small coordinator team that manages all of it simultaneously. Authorization requests pile up. Ancillary scheduling slips. Patients who should be following up in eight weeks drift to sixteen. Revenue that should have been captured disappears into the gap between intention and execution.

The neurology staffing reality makes this worse. Most practices have one or two coordinators handling scheduling, authorizations, referral management, test coordination, and patient communication across a panel of 1,500–3,000 patients. A single MS patient in an infusion cycle generates five to ten administrative touchpoints per month. Multiply that by the number of infusion patients and the math stops working.

The practices that fix this don't hire more coordinators. They change the system. Every authorization that can be templated gets templated. Every ancillary test that gets ordered gets tracked until the result arrives. Every chronic patient gets a follow-up touchpoint before they fall out of care. The work still happens — it just doesn't require a human to initiate every step.

clinIQ was built for this model. Not for the neurology practice running simple, low-complexity cases. For the practices managing the real mix: infusions, CGRP biologics, EMG/EEG coordination, seizure patients who need same-week access, and post-visit RTM billing that nobody has set up yet because the coordinator who was supposed to learn it left three months ago.

sections.h2: EMG and EEG Scheduling: Closing the Coordination Gap
sections.content:
EMG and EEG scheduling is a solved problem that most neurology practices have not solved. The physician orders the test at the end of a visit. The coordinator is supposed to schedule it. The patient is supposed to show up. The result is supposed to arrive before the follow-up appointment. None of this is complicated in isolation. In practice, it breaks at every handoff.

In-house EMG labs run into scheduling density problems. The test takes 45–90 minutes. You can fit four to six per day. The physician orders three in a morning clinic and now the next available slot is six weeks out — past the patient's follow-up date. The coordinator reschedules the follow-up without rescheduling the EMG first, and now the sequence is inverted.

External referrals for EEGs create a different set of problems. You send the order to the hospital neurology department or independent EEG center. The patient schedules on their own timeline. The result comes back in 10–14 days, sometimes longer. It arrives in the fax queue. Someone pulls it, scans it, attaches it to the chart. The physician sees it at the follow-up — or doesn't, if the follow-up was before the result arrived.

For ambulatory EEGs, the coordination burden is even higher. The patient picks up the equipment, wears it for 24–72 hours, returns it, the data uploads, a technologist reads it, the neurologist interprets it, and the result needs to reach the chart before the next appointment. There are seven steps and six opportunities for something to slip.

**What clinIQ does for EMG and EEG coordination:**

When a physician orders an EMG or EEG, clinIQ creates a tracking task automatically. The task lives in the coordinator dashboard with the patient name, test type, ordering date, and expected completion window.

For in-house testing, clinIQ flags scheduling conflicts before they happen. If the EMG order is placed and the patient's follow-up is in four weeks, the system identifies available EMG slots within the next two weeks and surfaces them to the coordinator. One click to schedule. No searching the calendar, no mental math on whether the result will arrive in time.

For external referrals, clinIQ tracks the referral status. When was the order sent? Has the external facility confirmed scheduling? Is the result expected before the follow-up appointment? If the result hasn't arrived three days before the follow-up, a coordinator alert fires. The coordinator can contact the facility, reschedule the follow-up, or notify the physician — rather than discovering the missing result when the patient is already in the room.

For ambulatory EEG, the equipment pickup and return windows are tracked. If the patient was supposed to return equipment on Tuesday and it's Thursday, the system flags it. The coordinator makes one call.

The operational result: neurologists stop walking into follow-up rooms without test results. Coordinators stop spending 45 minutes per day manually tracking pending EMG and EEG orders. Patients stop slipping through the gap between test order and result delivery.

For a practice ordering 30–50 EMGs and EEGs per month, the coordination time savings run 3–5 hours per week. That's time that was previously spent on hold with external facilities, digging through fax queues, and manually checking whether pending results had arrived.

sections.h2: Infusion Suite Management: MS, IVIG, and Botox
sections.content:
The neurology infusion suite is one of the highest-revenue-per-square-foot assets in a specialty practice — and one of the most underutilized. Most practices that run infusion suites operate them at 60–75% of theoretical capacity, not because patients aren't coming but because authorization delays, scheduling inefficiencies, and chair-turnover problems create gaps that compound across the month.

Multiple sclerosis infusion therapy drives the majority of infusion suite revenue in most neurology practices. Natalizumab (Tysabri) runs every four weeks. Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus) runs every six months, but the infusion itself takes 3.5 hours and requires 1 hour of post-infusion monitoring. Alemtuzumab (Lemtrada) runs in five-day and three-day cycles. Each drug has specific premedication requirements, vital sign monitoring schedules, and observation periods.

IVIG infusions for CIDP, myasthenia gravis, and other autoimmune conditions run 4–6 hours. The loading dose sequence may require multiple consecutive or closely spaced infusions. Maintenance doses typically run every 3–4 weeks.

Botox for chronic migraine runs every 12 weeks under the PREEMPT protocol — 31 injections across seven head and neck muscle groups, 155 units total. The prior authorization requirement is specific: documentation of 15 or more headache days per month for at least three months, with at least eight meeting migraine criteria, and failure of at least two preventive drug classes. Getting Botox covered requires building that documentation file in advance, not reconstructing it at authorization time.

**Authorization for infusion therapy:**

Every drug in the infusion suite has its own authorization requirements, and most require re-authorization at every cycle or every benefit period. Natalizumab requires documentation of RRMS diagnosis, prior DMT history, JC virus antibody status, and sometimes MRI findings. Ocrelizumab requires similar documentation plus B-cell monitoring. IVIG requires diagnosis-specific documentation and evidence of prior treatment response.

The practices losing infusion revenue to authorization delays have the same problem: they start the authorization process when the patient calls to schedule, instead of when the prior cycle ends. The turnaround time for infusion drug authorizations averages 5–14 days with most payers. If you start 10 days before the next scheduled infusion date, you have no buffer. Any request for additional documentation pushes the infusion past the clinical window.

clinIQ starts the next authorization cycle automatically when the current infusion is documented. For a monthly natalizumab patient, the next authorization request initiates 21 days before the scheduled date — enough time to submit, receive a denial, appeal, and still infuse on schedule. For Ocrevus at six-month intervals, the window is shorter, but the same logic applies.

**Chair scheduling and turnover:**

The infusion suite scheduling problem is different from clinic scheduling. Infusion appointments are measured in hours, not minutes. A 4-hour IVIG and a 45-minute Botox treatment cannot share the same chair slot. The chair assignment has to match the drug, the duration, and the monitoring requirements.

clinIQ builds infusion schedules around chair capacity and drug duration rather than appointment slots. When a coordinator schedules an ocrelizumab infusion, the system reserves 4.5 hours of chair time including post-infusion monitoring. It doesn't allow a 4-hour IVIG to be booked in the same chair starting 3 hours later. The schedule fills to actual capacity — not theoretical appointment slots.

For a 4-chair infusion suite running 8 hours per day, optimal scheduling yields 28–32 patient-hours of capacity. Most practices capture 18–22. The gap — 6–10 patient-hours — is recoverable revenue. At typical infusion reimbursement rates, each chair-hour represents $150–$400 in facility revenue depending on the drug. Closing 50% of the scheduling gap generates $450–$2,000 per day per suite.

sections.h2: Prior Authorization for High-Cost Neurology Medications
sections.content:
Neurology carries a higher prior authorization burden than almost any other specialty. CGRP inhibitors for chronic migraine, disease-modifying therapies for MS, enzyme replacement therapy for lysosomal storage diseases, and biologic therapies for autoimmune neuromuscular conditions all cost $10,000–$100,000+ per year. Every payer fights them on first submission.

The CGRP inhibitor class — erenumab (Aimovig), fremanezumab (Ajovy), galcanezumab (Emgality), eptinezumab (Vyepti), rimegepant (Nurtec), and atogepant (Qulipta) — requires step therapy through multiple oral preventive classes before approval. Most payers require documented failure of at least two or three of the following: propranolol or metoprolol, amitriptyline or nortriptyline, topiramate or valproate, and sometimes venlafaxine or an SNRI. The documentation requirement is specific: drug name, dose, duration, and reason for discontinuation or inadequate response.

The practices with high CGRP denial rates have a documentation problem, not an eligibility problem. The patient did fail propranolol — they remember trying it — but the 2019 prescription was at a different practice and the records were never obtained. The topiramate trial from the urgent care three years ago isn't in your system. The authorization is denied for incomplete step therapy documentation, even though the step therapy was completed.

**How clinIQ handles neurology medication authorization:**

For every patient started on a CGRP inhibitor, clinIQ opens a medication history file at the first visit. Headache frequency: logged at every visit with specific counts. Previous preventive trials: documented with drug, dose, duration, and outcome. The authorization request builds itself from the visit record — not from memory and not from reconstructed documentation at the time of submission.

For MS disease-modifying therapies, the authorization requirements vary by drug and by payer, but the core documentation is consistent: confirmed MS diagnosis with MRI findings, relapse history and frequency, prior DMT history with reason for transition, and current disability status. clinIQ maintains this file continuously, so when a patient switches from a first-line injectable to a high-efficacy oral agent or infusion therapy, the authorization submission goes out complete on day one.

For enzyme replacement therapies — agalsidase alfa for Fabry disease, alglucosidase alfa for Pompe disease, laronidase for MPS I — the authorization documentation requirements are extensive and the approval process often involves specialty pharmacy programs, REMS enrollment, and manufacturer patient support programs. clinIQ tracks each component of the approval process and flags delays before they affect the treatment schedule.

**The appeal process:**

For denials, clinIQ generates the appeal letter from the authorization file. The appeal includes the original submission documentation, the denial reason, and point-by-point rebuttal with references to clinical guidelines. For CGRP inhibitors denied for step therapy, the appeal cites the AHS/AAN consensus statement on preventive migraine treatment and documents each failed preventive trial with dates and doses. First-level appeals that arrive fully documented have a significantly higher overturn rate than appeals submitted as simple reconsideration requests.

For peer-to-peer reviews, clinIQ generates the physician brief — a one-page summary of the patient's history, the authorization rationale, and the clinical guideline support for the requested medication. Neurologists report that peer-to-peer calls run 8–12 minutes when the physician has the brief in front of them. Without preparation, the same calls run 25–30 minutes and are less likely to succeed.

sections.h2: Seizure Disorder and Urgent Access Management
sections.content:
Epilepsy and seizure disorder management creates a scheduling problem that doesn't fit standard appointment templates. Breakthrough seizures are urgent. A patient who has had a seizure after being seizure-free for eight months needs to be seen within 48–72 hours, not scheduled for the next available appointment in four weeks. The physician needs to see the patient, review the medication level if relevant, assess for triggers or changes in compliance, and decide whether to adjust the regimen.

At the same time, seizure patients need regular scheduled follow-ups to maintain seizure control and medication monitoring. Anti-seizure medications — levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate, oxcarbazepine, lacosamide, and others — require periodic lab monitoring. Valproate requires LFTs and CBC. Some patients require therapeutic drug levels. Pregnancy-capable patients on valproate require specific counseling and monitoring. The routine monitoring isn't optional — it's the standard of care.

Most neurology practices manage this by keeping a few urgent slots open per week. The problem is that urgent slot utilization is unpredictable. Some weeks no one has a breakthrough seizure. Other weeks three patients break through and there are only two slots. The standard response is to overbook the schedule on urgent days, which creates a cascade of delays that affects every patient in clinic that day.

**How clinIQ manages seizure disorder access:**

clinIQ categorizes patients by acuity based on seizure history and current control status. Patients who are seizure-free on stable medications are standard follow-up. Patients who have had a breakthrough seizure in the past six months, who are in medication titration, or who have a history of status epilepticus are flagged as high-priority access.

For high-priority patients, clinIQ maintains same-week access slots that are released to regular scheduling only if not claimed by 48 hours before the slot. When a high-priority patient calls or messages with a breakthrough seizure, the system identifies available urgent slots and offers them first — before the coordinator manually searches the schedule.

For medication monitoring, clinIQ runs automated lab due-date tracking. If a patient on valproate is due for their quarterly LFT and CBC, the system generates a lab order at the visit prior to the due date and includes it in the after-visit summary. If the lab result doesn't arrive within the expected window, a coordinator alert fires. The alert doesn't require the coordinator to remember which patients are on which medications and when monitoring is due — the system maintains that list.

**Driving restriction counseling and documentation:**

Physicians have a legal and ethical obligation to counsel seizure patients on driving restrictions, and the counseling needs to be documented. State laws vary on seizure-free intervals required before driving resumption — California requires six months, other states vary from three months to no mandated interval. clinIQ includes driving restriction documentation in the seizure disorder follow-up template. When a patient has a breakthrough seizure, the note template prompts the driving restriction discussion and documents the state-specific guidance provided. The documentation is in the chart. The physician's obligation is fulfilled. The liability risk is reduced.

sections.h2: RTM Billing for Chronic Neurology Patients
sections.content:
Remote Therapeutic Monitoring (RTM) represents one of the most underutilized revenue streams in neurology. Chronic neurology patients — chronic migraine, MS, epilepsy, CIDP, Parkinson's disease — are exactly the population RTM was designed for. They have chronic conditions requiring ongoing symptom monitoring. They benefit from between-visit touchpoints. And most neurology practices are billing almost none of it.

The RTM CPT codes for neurology:

**CPT 98975** — RTM device supply with scheduled recording and transmission, musculoskeletal system; initial set-up and patient education. $19–23/month.

**CPT 98980** — RTM treatment management, first 20 minutes of qualified healthcare professional time in a calendar month. $50–60/month.

**CPT 98981** — RTM treatment management, each additional 20 minutes in a calendar month. $40–50/month.

For a neurology practice with 100 chronic patients enrolled in RTM, the monthly revenue potential is $7,000–$12,000 from 98975 and 98980 alone, before any additional 98981 billing. Annualized, that's $84,000–$144,000 in revenue that does not require an additional physician visit.

**Why most neurology practices are not billing RTM:**

The setup friction is high if you're doing it manually. You need to identify eligible patients. You need to have a conversation with each patient about the program. You need to provide the monitoring mechanism — whether that's a disease-specific app, a digital symptom diary, or a connected device. You need to document 20 minutes of clinical staff time per patient per month reviewing the transmitted data. You need to make sure the billing codes are attached to the right visit or encounter. Most practices start RTM with good intentions, get through the initial patient enrollment, and then watch the billing drift as the month-to-month management overwhelms the coordinator.

**How clinIQ runs RTM for neurology:**

Patient identification is automatic. When a patient with a chronic neurology diagnosis — migraine (G43.x), MS (G35), epilepsy (G40.x), Parkinson's (G20), CIDP (G61.81) — is seen in clinic, clinIQ flags them as RTM-eligible. The coordinator gets a prompt at checkout: this patient qualifies for remote monitoring. Would you like to enroll them today?

The patient education happens at checkout. The coordinator reviews what the monitoring involves — for migraine patients, daily headache diary tracking with rescue medication usage; for epilepsy patients, seizure frequency logging; for MS patients, fatigue and function scores. The setup is documented as the 98975 encounter.

Each month, clinIQ surfaces the RTM review queue. The clinical staff opens the queue, reviews the submitted data for each patient, identifies any patients whose data suggests a clinical change that needs physician attention, and documents the review. The 20-minute clock runs within the system. When 20 minutes is reached, the 98980 code is ready to bill. If additional time is spent, 98981 captures it.

For Parkinson's patients, RTM monitoring can include motor diary data from connected devices or app-based tracking of tremor, off-time, and medication timing. This data is clinically useful for medication adjustment and is billable under the same RTM framework.

For chronic migraine patients, headache diary data tracked between visits replaces the retrospective recall that dominates most migraine follow-up visits. Instead of asking how many headache days the patient thinks they had last month, the neurologist reviews the diary. The visit is more efficient. The documentation is more accurate. And the between-visit touchpoint — when the staff reviews the diary data and identifies a patient whose headache frequency has increased — catches deterioration before it becomes a crisis visit.

Neurology practices implementing clinIQ follow a structured four-week onboarding process that prioritizes revenue impact first.

**Week 1 — Authorization and infusion setup:**

The team uploads payer-specific authorization templates for the medications most commonly prescribed in the practice: CGRP inhibitors, MS DMTs, and infusion drugs. Existing patients in active authorization cycles are imported. The infusion suite scheduling rules are configured — chair count, operating hours, drug-specific duration defaults. By end of week one, new authorization requests generate from templates and the infusion schedule reflects real capacity.

**Week 2 — Ancillary test coordination:**

EMG and EEG order tracking goes live. All pending ancillary orders are entered into the system. The coordinator team starts receiving alerts for tests that don't have results within the expected window. External facility contacts are loaded. The reflex is trained: when a test is ordered, it goes into the system; when a result is expected and hasn't arrived, the system tells you.

**Week 3 — RTM enrollment:**

Eligible chronic patients are identified from the active panel. The coordinator team begins enrollment conversations at checkout. The goal for week three is 20–30 enrolled patients. The monitoring mechanism is selected for each diagnosis category. The billing workflow is tested end-to-end for the first enrolled patients.

**Week 4 — Full operation and seizure access management:**

The urgent access protocol for seizure patients is activated. High-priority patients are flagged. Urgent slots are configured. The lab monitoring schedule for anti-seizure medications is loaded. The practice is running on full clinical logic.

**Revenue impact timeline:**

Most neurology practices see measurable revenue impact within the first billing cycle. Authorization approval rates improve immediately because submissions go out complete. Infusion suite utilization increases within the first month as scheduling gaps close. RTM billing builds across 90 days as the patient panel enrolls.

A practice with a 4-chair infusion suite running at 70% utilization captures approximately $15,000–$25,000 per month in infusion facility revenue. Closing the utilization gap to 90% adds $4,000–$8,000 per month. The authorization improvement alone — reducing the 3–5 staff hours per authorization denial — recaptures 15–20 staff hours per month that can be redirected to enrollment and patient communication.

For RTM, the 90-day enrollment ramp typically reaches 80–120 patients for a practice with 1,500–2,500 active chronic patients. At that enrollment level, RTM billing runs $8,000–$14,000 per month on an ongoing basis with minimal incremental staff time after the initial enrollment period.

Total annualized impact for a mid-size neurology practice (2–3 physicians, 4-chair infusion suite, 2,000 active patients): $180,000–$280,000 in recovered and new revenue within 12 months.

faqs.q: Does clinIQ work with our existing EHR for neurology documentation?
faqs.a: Yes. clinIQ integrates with major EHR platforms used in neurology including Epic, Athenahealth, eClinicalWorks, and Modernizing Medicine. The integration is bidirectional — patient demographics, diagnoses, and orders flow into clinIQ, and authorization records, RTM documentation, and scheduling data sync back to the EHR. You do not replace your EHR. You add the coordination and automation layer on top of it.

faqs.q: How does clinIQ handle the step therapy documentation for CGRP inhibitors?
faqs.a: clinIQ maintains a running medication history file for each chronic migraine patient from the first visit. Every preventive medication trial is documented with drug name, dose, duration, and reason for discontinuation or inadequate response. When authorization is requested for a CGRP inhibitor, the step therapy documentation pulls directly from this file. The submission goes out complete — no reconstruction, no missing trial documentation. For patients transferring from another practice, clinIQ includes an intake checklist to capture prior medication history at the first visit.

faqs.q: Can clinIQ manage the JC virus monitoring requirements for natalizumab patients?
faqs.a: Yes. For natalizumab patients, clinIQ tracks JC virus antibody testing intervals (every six months for antibody-positive patients, every twelve months for antibody-negative). The system generates lab order reminders before the due date and flags patients who are overdue. For patients approaching the 24-dose threshold — where PML risk increases significantly for antibody-positive patients — clinIQ flags the upcoming threshold and prompts the risk-benefit discussion documentation.

faqs.q: How does RTM billing work for neurology patients who don't have a connected device?
faqs.a: RTM does not require a connected device. The CMS definition of RTM includes patient-generated health data transmitted through qualified communication technology, which includes app-based symptom diaries, digital headache logs, and seizure frequency tracking tools. clinIQ provides app-based monitoring tools for common neurology diagnoses — headache diary, seizure log, fatigue and function tracker — that satisfy the RTM transmission requirement. Patients without smartphones can use a structured telephone check-in that meets the monitoring requirements under certain code descriptions.

faqs.q: How do you handle urgent scheduling for breakthrough seizure patients without disrupting the rest of the clinic schedule?
faqs.a: clinIQ maintains a reserve of 2–4 urgent slots per week that are visible in the scheduling system as available but are tagged as urgent-priority. When a breakthrough seizure patient contacts the practice, the coordinator accesses the urgent slot pool first. These slots are released to routine scheduling 48 hours before the appointment if not claimed by an urgent patient. The result is predictable access for urgent neurology patients without routine overbooking, which reduces the cascade delays that frustrate the entire clinic when urgent cases displace scheduled patients.

faqs.q: What is the typical authorization approval rate for MS infusion drugs after implementing clinIQ?
faqs.a: Practices using clinIQ's neurology authorization templates see first-submission approval rates of 82–88% for MS infusion drugs compared to an industry average of 55–65%. The improvement comes from two sources: complete submission documentation that eliminates the most common denial reasons, and early authorization initiation that provides enough lead time to appeal and re-submit before the scheduled infusion date. For drugs with REMS programs, clinIQ tracks REMS enrollment status and includes it in the authorization package automatically.

faqs.q: Can clinIQ track the PREEMPT protocol requirements for Botox chronic migraine authorization?
faqs.a: Yes. clinIQ includes a chronic migraine tracking template that documents headache frequency (total headache days and migraine days per month) at every visit, cumulative headache day data since initial presentation, and all acute and preventive medications tried with duration and outcome. The PREEMPT authorization package generates from this data — including the 15 headache days per month threshold, the eight migraine days per month threshold, and the two failed oral preventive classes. The submission is built to match payer-specific PREEMPT criteria.

faqs.q: How long does it take to see RTM revenue after implementing clinIQ?
faqs.a: The first RTM billing cycle typically occurs within 30 days of implementation. The enrollment ramp — getting patients identified, consented, and actively transmitting data — runs 60–90 days to reach meaningful scale. Practices that prioritize RTM enrollment during weeks 3–4 of implementation typically reach 50–80 enrolled patients by the end of month two and 100–150 by month three. At 100 enrolled patients billing 98975 and 98980, monthly RTM revenue runs $8,000–$12,000.

testimonial.quote: We were spending 12–15 hours a week chasing EMG results, re-submitting CGRP authorizations, and manually tracking our Tysabri patients' JC virus labs. clinIQ automated all of it. Our coordinator now spends that time enrolling patients in RTM, which added $10,000 a month in revenue we weren't capturing before.
